name: Sundarbans National Park
shortDescription: World's largest mangrove forest — last stronghold of the Royal Bengal Tiger
description: The Sundarbans, spanning 10,000 square kilometres across India and Bangladesh, is the world's largest continuous mangrove forest and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. It is the only mangrove forest in the world where tigers swim between islands — the Royal Bengal Tiger population here has adapted entirely to life in the tidal delta. Eco-tourism is strictly regulated: all boat safaris are limited to 20 vessels per day in core zones, operated by former fishermen who were retrained as wildlife guides under a government scheme that converted 1,200 poachers into conservationists. Revenue from the Sundarban Tiger Reserve goes directly into the Village Conservation and Development Programme supporting 102 villages at the forest edge.
highlights: ["UNESCO World Heritage Site \u2014 10,000 sq km mangrove", "Royal Bengal Tigers that swim between islands", "1,200 former poachers now licensed wildlife guides", "Village Conservation Programme \u2014 102 edge villages", "Irrawaddy dolphins and saltwater crocodiles", "20-vessel daily cap on core zone boat safaris"]

name: Kaziranga National Park
shortDescription: Home to two-thirds of the world's one-horned rhino population
description: Kaziranga National Park in Assam hosts more than 2,600 Indian one-horned rhinoceroses — approximately 70% of the entire global population of this species. The park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, was created in 1905 after Viceroy Curzon's wife saw only one rhino during a visit and demanded immediate protection. Today the rhino population has recovered from near extinction, largely due to the bravery of the park's anti-poaching forest guards, who have the highest shoot-on-sight mandate in any Indian protected area. Elephant safaris at dawn offer the most intimate wildlife experience available in India — you move silently through tall elephant grass at height. All mahouts are from the Mishing tribe and receive 30% of safari revenue directly.
highlights: ["70% of world's one-horned rhino population", "UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1985", "Dawn elephant safaris through tall grass", "Mishing tribe mahouts earn 30% of all revenue", "Highest tiger density in India", "Asian elephants, wild buffalo, swamp deer"]

name: Rann of Kutch Wildlife Sanctuary
shortDescription: The world's largest salt desert — home to the last wild Indian asses
description: The Rann of Kutch is a seasonal salt marsh in Gujarat spanning 23,300 square kilometres, forming the world's largest salt desert when dry in winter. The Indian Wild Ass Sanctuary within the Rann is the last habitat of the Khur — the Indian wild ass — with a population that has recovered from 362 individuals in 1960 to over 6,000 today. The surreal white landscape at full moon is one of the most otherworldly sights in Asia. The Rann Utsav festival, held from November to February, showcases Kutchi folk music, embroidery, and cuisine in an eco-tent village built and managed entirely by Kutchi artisan communities. All embroidery purchased at the festival is certified by the Kutch Craft Foundation to ensure artisan provenance.
highlights: ["Last habitat of Indian wild ass — recovered to 6,000", "World's largest salt desert — 23,300 sq km", "Full moon white desert experience", "Rann Utsav — eco-tent festival by Kutchi communities", "Kutch Craft Foundation certified embroidery", "Flamingo flocks and migratory birds"]

name: Nagarhole National Park
shortDescription: Karnataka's premier tiger and elephant reserve — part of the Nilgiri Biosphere
description: Nagarhole National Park, part of the Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve — the largest protected forest block in India — is considered by wildlife photographers to be India's finest wildlife destination for visibility. The teak forests and grassy swamps of the Kabini River create exceptional conditions for spotting tigers, leopards, Indian elephants, and gaur. The Kabini backwaters turn gold at sunset, and during summer when water is scarce, concentration of animals at the river is extraordinary. The Jenu Kuruba and Betta Kuruba tribal communities have traditional forest rights within the park, and a portion of every tourism permit fee funds their Forest Rights Act entitlements.
highlights: ["Part of Nilgiri Biosphere — India's largest forest block", "Kabini River backwaters — golden sunset safaris", "Summer dry season: highest wildlife concentration", "Tigers, leopards, elephants and gaur regularly sighted", "Tribal forest rights fund from permit revenue", "India's top destination for wildlife photographers"]

name: Coorg Organic Coffee Estates
shortDescription: India's coffee country — shade-grown estates under a canopy of silver oaks
description: The Kodagu (Coorg) district produces 30% of India's entire coffee output, all grown under a shade canopy of silver oak trees in a system that has maintained forest cover across nearly 280,000 hectares. Unlike sun-grown monoculture coffee, Coorg's traditional shade-grown estates support extraordinary biodiversity — over 310 bird species have been recorded within active coffee estates. Many estates have been Rainforest Alliance certified for their role in maintaining this forest-agriculture mosaic. Homestays within working estates give visitors full access to the coffee production cycle — from cherry-picking in October to pulping, drying, and cupping. The indigenous Kodava community manages most of the estates under a cooperative structure.
highlights: ["Produces 30% of India's coffee — all shade-grown", "310+ bird species within active coffee estates", "Rainforest Alliance certified estates", "Coffee cherry-to-cup farm experience", "Kodava indigenous community cooperative ownership", "280,000 hectares of continuous shade canopy"]

name: Lonar Crater Lake
shortDescription: World's only basaltic impact crater filled with a hyper-saline alkaline lake
description: Lonar in Buldhana district is a National Geo-Heritage Monument and one of only four hyper-velocity meteorite impact craters on basaltic rock in the world. The 1.8-km wide crater was formed approximately 50,000 years ago. The saline alkaline lake within — with a pH of 10.5 — contains unique extremophile bacteria found nowhere else, making it a subject of active NASA research. The crater rim is lined with 14 temples from the Chalukya and Hemadpanthi periods, and the dense forest within the crater walls is a Wildlife Sanctuary home to peacocks, flamingos, and migratory waterfowl. This remains one of India's most undervisited geo-heritage sites.
highlights: ["One of four basaltic meteorite craters on earth", "NASA research site \u2014 unique extremophile bacteria", "pH 10.5 hyper-saline alkaline lake", "14 Chalukya temples on crater rim", "Wildlife Sanctuary \u2014 peacocks and flamingos", "National Geo-Heritage Monument"]

name: Longwa — Village on the Border
shortDescription: The chief's house straddles the India-Myanmar border — you can breakfast in two countries
description: Longwa village in Mon district, Nagaland, sits precisely on the international border between India and Myanmar. The chief's longhouse — home of the Angh of the Konyak Naga tribe — is divided by the border, meaning the chief sleeps in Myanmar and breakfasts in India. The Konyak Nagas were the last head-hunting tribe in the world, with the last recorded head-hunt in 1969. The village elders carry visible facial tattoos that indicate the number of heads taken — they are among the last humans on earth with such markings. The surrounding forest is part of a community-protected area in which all hunting was voluntarily banned in 1985. Homestays are entirely community-managed.
highlights: ["Chief's house straddles India-Myanmar border", "Konyak Naga — last head-hunting tribe (ended 1969)", "Village elders with traditional facial tattoos", "Community forest ban on hunting since 1985", "Breakfast in India, sleep in Myanmar", "Entirely community-managed homestays"]

name: Agumbe Rainforest Research Station
shortDescription: The Cherrapunji of the South — India's last refuge of the king cobra
description: Agumbe in the Malnad region of Karnataka receives the second-highest rainfall in India after Cherrapunji, earning it the title 'Cherrapunji of the South.' The Agumbe Rainforest Research Station, founded by herpetologist Romulus Whitaker, is the world's only dedicated king cobra research facility. The station has radio-tracked over 60 king cobras in the wild and operates a citizen science program welcoming volunteers from around the world. The evergreen rainforest here is a UNESCO-listed biodiversity hotspot containing lion-tailed macaques, slender lorises, and the Malabar giant squirrel. Guided night trails are available only with ARRS naturalists — no commercial operators.
highlights: ["World's only king cobra dedicated research station", "Second highest rainfall in India", "Radio-tracked king cobra citizen science program", "Lion-tailed macaque and slender loris habitat", "UNESCO Western Ghats biodiversity hotspot", "Night trails with ARRS naturalists only"]

name: Chettinad Mansion Heritage Trail
shortDescription: 75 palatial mansions built by India's most successful merchant community
description: The Chettinad region of Tamil Nadu, comprising 75 villages, was the homeland of the Nattukotai Chettiar merchant community who dominated trade across Southeast Asia in the 19th century. With their accumulated wealth they built extraordinary mansions combining Italian marble, Burmese teak, Belgian glass, and South Indian stonework in a synthesis found nowhere else on earth. Many of these mansions are now heritage hotels run by Chettiar family descendants. The cuisine of Chettinad — considered the most complex spice-layered cuisine in South India — can be learned in cooking masterclasses conducted in heritage kitchens. The Chettinad Heritage Trust certifies all accommodation and cuisine experiences to ensure authentic community benefit.
highlights: ["75 palatial mansions with Italian marble and Burmese teak", "Chettinad cuisine masterclasses in heritage kitchens", "Nattukotai Chettiar Heritage Trust certification", "Antique shops with verified provenance items", "Colonial-era karaikudi tiles \u2014 still handmade here", "Family-run heritage hotels in original mansions"]

name: Shekhawati Open Air Art Gallery
shortDescription: The world's largest open-air art gallery — 5,000 painted havelis in the Rajasthan desert
description: The Shekhawati region of Rajasthan, comprising the towns of Nawalgarh, Mandawa, Fatehpur, Jhunjhunu, and Bikaner, contains the largest concentration of painted havelis (merchant mansions) in the world. Over 5,000 buildings are covered in exquisitely detailed frescoes painted between 1830 and 1930 by the Marwari merchant community, depicting scenes that include early aeroplanes, trains, and motor cars — technologies the artists had never seen, painted from travellers' descriptions. The Shekhawati Heritage Trust trains local young painters in fresco restoration techniques, creating a living craft tradition that supports 200 artist families. Walking tours are led by ASI-registered guides.
highlights: ["World's largest open-air art gallery — 5,000 havelis", "Frescoes depicting trains and planes from descriptions", "Shekhawati Heritage Trust fresco restoration program", "200 local artist families in active restoration", "Walking tours with ASI-registered guides", "Intact 19th century merchant town layouts"]

name: Champaner-Pavagadh Heritage
shortDescription: UNESCO Heritage site combining Hindu temples and Sultanate mosques
description: Champaner-Pavagadh Archaeological Park is a UNESCO World Heritage Site in Gujarat containing the only pre-Mughal Islamic capital in India that remains unaltered and intact. The site combines the 11th century Hindu temples on Pavagadh hill — still active pilgrimage sites — with the 15th century Sultanate capital of Mahmud Begada below, creating a unique layered heritage landscape. The Jama Masjid at Champaner, with its 172 pillars and exquisite stone lattice work, is considered the finest Gujarati Sultanate mosque in existence. A 40-minute ropeway connects the base to the Pavagadh temple summit. The Archaeological Survey's Champaner Conservation Centre trains local stonemasons in traditional heritage restoration.
highlights: ["UNESCO World Heritage \u2014 only intact pre-Mughal capital", "Jama Masjid \u2014 172-pillar Sultanate masterpiece", "Active Hindu pilgrimage temples on Pavagadh hill", "ASI Conservation Centre trains local stonemasons", "Ropeway to 11th century Kalika Mata Temple", "11th-15th century layers visible simultaneously"]

name: Orchha Medieval Town
shortDescription: A perfectly preserved medieval Bundela capital where time stopped in 1783
description: Orchha, on the banks of the Betwa River, was the capital of the Bundela Rajput kingdom from 1531 and was effectively abandoned in 1783 when the capital moved to Tikamgarh. This abrupt departure preserved the entire medieval town in an extraordinary state — palaces, temples, cenotaphs, and bazaars remain almost exactly as they were 250 years ago. The Ram Raja Temple here is unique in India as the only temple where Lord Ram is worshipped as a king: the deity receives a military salute from the state police every morning at sunrise. The Betwa River is a protected eco-zone with crocodile nesting sites on its banks. Local guides from the Bundela Heritage Foundation have exclusive guiding rights, preventing outside commercial operators.
highlights: ["Medieval capital preserved exactly as left in 1783", "Ram Raja Temple \u2014 only temple giving state police salute", "14 cenotaphs on Betwa River bathing ghats", "Betwa River \u2014 protected crocodile nesting habitat", "Bundela Heritage Foundation exclusive guiding rights", "Chaturbhuj Temple \u2014 7-storey tower of pure devotion"]
